weather: Today in Edmonds: Overcast and cool with misty conditions. Current temp 47°F, feels like 43°F. High 56°F, low 45°F. South winds 5-10 mph. No precipitation expected today. Skies will clear slightly tonight, becoming partly cloudy. Typical spring weather - dress warmly and expect damp conditions.

local_news.findings: Edmonds will begin a major sewer and storm pipe rehabilitation project on May 4, targeting 27 locations with failing infrastructure identified in a 2013 review. The $419,000 project will use cured-in-place pipe (CIPP) technology to repair aging sewer and storm pipes across the city. Construction will occur at 27 locations and is expected to last several weeks.

The City of Edmonds has reached the 60% design level for Stage 3 of the Highway 99 Revitalization Project and is soliciting public feedback through an Urban Design Survey. The public is invited to complete the survey to help shape the final design for this major transportation corridor improvement project.

Volunteer applications for the 2026 Edmonds Art Festival are now open. The festival, scheduled for July 19-21, is seeking volunteers to help with this beloved community tradition. This marks the second year for the Sketcher Fest Edmonds event.

Edmonds Police are searching for suspects who assaulted and robbed two women walking on a local trail. The incident occurred recently, and police are actively investigating the case while seeking information from the public.

Paid-permit parking will begin May 1 at Shoreline North, Shoreline South, and Northgate Sound Transit parking garages. Single-occupant vehicles will need permits to park at these transit facilities, affecting local commuters who use these stations for transportation.

career.notes: Most districts are using pool postings for 2026-27 anticipated vacancies rather than single specific openings. Everett, Snohomish and Northshore list salary ranges; Edmonds posts ranges on secondary sources. Lake Washington and Bellevue encourage applicants to monitor general pools and apply to individual postings as they appear April–June. All require valid WA state teaching certificate with appropriate endorsements.

family.choir: Seattle Pro Musica has concluded 2025-26 auditions, but invites singers to email auditions@seattlepromusica.org to join the August 2026-27 season mailing list. Choral Arts Northwest will hold 2026-27 season auditions in July/August 2026—contact miriam@choralartsnw.org to schedule. Pacific Lutheran University’s Choral Union posts ensemble auditions on August 9, 10, 23, and 24, 2026.
family.toddler: May 2026 toddler highlights: Lynnwood Library offers Toddler Storytime with Nathalie on Monday, May 4, 9:30–10:00 am, and with Heather on Tuesday, May 5, 11:00–11:30 am. Edmonds Library lists Baby Storytime for 0–18 months and Preschool Storytime for 3–5 years. Imagine Children’s Museum in Everett provides daily hands-on exhibits perfect for 2-year-olds, plus special WonderFest activities on May 10.

global_news.findings: The United Arab Emirates announced it will exit OPEC, delivering a major blow to the global oil cartel while US-Iran ceasefire negotiations remain stalled. The UAE's departure from OPEC marks a significant shift in global oil politics, coming as the Trump administration appears unlikely to accept Iran's offer to reopen the Strait of Hormuz. This development could further destabilize oil markets already strained by the Iran conflict.

A $500 million Russian superyacht named Nord, linked to sanctioned billionaire Alexei Mordashov, successfully sailed through the Strait of Hormuz despite the ongoing blockade. The 142-meter vessel traveled from Dubai to Oman over the weekend, becoming one of the few private ships to transit the critical waterway in recent months. The transit raises questions about enforcement of sanctions and the blockade's effectiveness.

Google received guidance from EU antitrust regulators on how to help rival AI companies access its services under the Digital Markets Act. The European Commission proposed measures to ensure competing AI services can effectively interact with applications on Android devices, potentially reducing Google's dominance in mobile AI services. This marks a significant development in EU tech regulation efforts.

The US Department of Homeland Security announced new policies to vet immigrants for what it calls 'extremist views,' raising significant free speech concerns. Under the Trump administration's guidance, immigrants can be denied green cards for expressing political opinions like participating in pro-Palestinian protests or criticizing Israel. Rights advocates warn this could stifle First Amendment protections and due process rights.

King Charles III began his four-day US state visit, arriving for a ceremonial welcome at the White House. The King is scheduled to address Congress on Tuesday, where he is expected to defend democratic values and express sympathy over the recent assassination attempt on President Trump. The visit comes amid strained US-UK relations and ongoing political tensions.

A man pleaded guilty to planning a terrorist attack on a Taylor Swift concert in Vienna, highlighting ongoing security concerns for high-profile entertainment events. The guilty plea comes as authorities worldwide remain vigilant against potential threats to public gatherings and cultural events.

intellectual_journals.findings: Ian Penman revisits the Beach Boys not as nostalgic surf-pop relics but as accidental theologians of American loneliness.  Brian Wilson’s miniature three-minute cathedrals—‘Don’t Talk (Put Your Head on My Shoulder)’, ‘Caroline, No’, ‘I Just Wasn’t Made for These Times’—are treated as secular hymns whose chord progressions “go toward God” without ever naming him.  Penman argues that the band’s real achievement was to preserve the sacred tension between Saturday-night release and Sunday-morning longing that gave early rock its metaphysical voltage, a tension later flattened by the culture into e [TRUNCATED]

Flora Champy defends reading as an end in itself against the utilitarian moralism that treats books as either self-help tools or networking accessories.  Taking Proust’s 1905 essay On Reading as her touchstone, she argues that literature’s highest gift is not ethical improvement but the preservation of fugitive experience: books keep past sensations alive without pretending to redeem them.  Ruskin’s Victorian worry—that reading should train disinterested attention rather than serve social advancement—becomes, in Champy’s hands, a diagnosis of our own platform age, where algorithmic feeds train [TRUNCATED]

Maria Popova turns the physics of bird flight into a meditation on vulnerability and collective intelligence.  Drawing on Alan Lightman’s new essay collection, she explains how the V-formation is not an aerodynamic gimmick but an evolved ethics: each bird momentarily sacrifices optimal individual lift to reduce drag on the flock, trusting that the formation will rotate and repay the favor.  The essay widens into a reflection on human social architectures—families, democracies, scientific collaborations—that likewise depend on the willingness to bear extra weight so that others may glide.  Popo [TRUNCATED]

Will Alden reports from Los Angeles on the Iranian diaspora’s rehearsal for regime change.  At a lakeside Easter picnic in the San Fernando Valley, supporters of exiled crown prince Reza Pahlavi chant “Long Live the Shah” beside hookah smoke and EDM remixes of revolutionary slogans, waiting for Washington’s war to hand them what they call “day zero.”  The Constitutionalist Party of Iran, founded in 1994 by royal ministers in exile, has turned weekly Westwood rallies into a disciplined political machine, complete with Trump-Pahlavi posters and a generational split between hardline monarchists a [TRUNCATED]

triadic_ontology.findings: Matteo Raffaelli's new article in Modern Theology (2026) positions the TRIERTIUM Research Group's Trinitarian ontology—rooted in Comenius' 17th-century pansophy and the Czech Trinitarian school—as a living response to modernity's spiritual and cultural challenges. In parallel, Christopher Isabelle's April 2026 paper "Triadic Relational Ontology: From Primordial Separation to Relational Field" argues that dyadic structures (self/other, true/false) are necessary but insufficient for emergence and meaning; a third, actively-maintained relational field is required, without which intelligibility collapses rather than degrades. Most striking is the April 15, 2026 Zenodo thesis "The Triadic Closure of Existence: Co-Emergence of Binding, Transformation, and Relational Readout," which proposes that the universe does not compile sequentially but emerges through simultaneous, phase-locked triadic closure. The three fundamental forces are not additive layers but non-negotiable closure roles—stable [TRUNCATED]

ai_systems.findings: The autonomous research landscape has shifted decisively toward centralized cognitive cores over sprawling agent swarms. Google’s PaperOrchestra (April 2026) demonstrates the new standard: five specialized agents—outline, literature-review, figure, citation, and LaTeX-compiler—operate under a single orchestrator that makes ~6,070 LLM calls to turn raw lab notes into a submission-ready manuscript. This mirrors the broader “re-centralization” trend: Google Research found that multi-agent architectures degrade performance by 39–70 % on sequential tasks, pushing teams to fold formerly separate agents into one reasoning core. Autoscience, fresh off a $14 M seed round led by General Catalyst, is scaling this approach with Mira, a commercial successor to its peer-review-winning Carl system. Mira spins up hundreds of short-lived research agents in parallel, explores unconventional ML architectures, then folds the survivors back into a unified cognitive model—delivering the throughput of a 100- [TRUNCATED]

uap.findings: Congressional UAP disclosure efforts accelerated dramatically in 2026. President Trump directed the Pentagon to begin releasing classified UFO files in February, with Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth leading the effort. The House Oversight Task Force, chaired by Rep. Anna Paulina Luna, continues investigating AARO's transparency failures and has demanded video files of UAP sightings. Rep. Tim Burchett claims classified briefings contain "earth-shattering" evidence that would "set the country unglued." The FY2026 NDAA includes three major UAP provisions requiring Pentagon briefings on military intercepts since 2004. A classified March 25 SCIF briefing sparked controversy following the mysterious disappearance of retired Air Force General Neil McCasland, who oversaw classified aerospace programs. Former intelligence officials testified that non-human biologics were recovered from UAP crash sites, while the Senate's UAP Disclosure Act amendment mandates creation of a National Archives colle [TRUNCATED]

newyorker.title: Ellen Burstyn’s Inner Library
newyorker.dek: Kris Kristofferson told her he was a poet when they co-starred in “Alice Doesn’t Live Here Anymore.” Her new book tells the story of her life in poetry, Michael Schulman writes.
newyorker.text:
She wore green slacks, a green turtleneck, and a green cardigan (“I do like green”), with chunky metallic necklaces. In her writing room, lined with shelves of Mary Oliver and Rumi—her Oscar, for “Alice Doesn’t Live Here Anymore,” was across the hall—she showed off the desk where she wrote her new book, “Poetry Says It Better,” in which she revisits her life story through verses she has loved. “I like what metaphors set off in your mind,” she said. “I like what poetry plays with.”
She settled into a wicker chair in the living room, beneath a wooden mermaid that she got in Mexico. Her cavapoo, Kerri, sat regally on a divan. The first poem that spoke to Burstyn, she recalled, was William Ernest Henley’s “Invictus,” which she read in high school, in Detroit. “ ‘I am the master of my fate, I am the captain of my soul’—that awakened an understanding in me that life was not always going to be the way it was.” At school, she was head cheerleader, but her home life was arduous. “My mother married a man who hated her two children,” she said. “I was being punished a lot physically.” She wrote her own poem and gave it to a boy in her class. “He said, ‘Just because your name is Edna’—which was my original name—‘doesn’t mean you have to copy her style.’ I said, ‘Who?’ ” That’s how she discovered Edna St. Vincent Millay. “It was like she was my voice,” she said, and rattled off Millay’s sonnet “Love Is Not All.”
At nineteen, Burstyn married her first husband, a poet and car salesman (“In Detroit, everybody’s involved with cars”), who introduced her to Baudelaire. “When I read Baudelaire now, I go, Boy, he’s really dark!” she said. By twenty-four, she was modelling in New York and decided to become an actress. “I went around saying, ‘I’m going to do a Broadway play this fall. Do you know how I can get an audition?’ ” It worked: she made her Broadway début in 1957, in the comedy “Fair Game.” Her determination put her in mind of the poem “For Longing,” by John O’Donohue: “May you have the courage to listen to the voice of desire.”
Some years later, in Hollywood, she was acting in the Debbie Reynolds flick “Goodbye Charlie.” “I said, ‘This is it. This is the big time. Next I’ll be playing Debbie Reynolds’s part!’ And this voice in my head said, I don’t want it,” she recalled. Through Shelley Winters, she met the acting teacher Lee Strasberg and studied with him. “I did a scene for him, and he said, ‘I don’t know, dear. You either laugh or you cry. If you would laugh and cry at the same time, then we’d see something.’ ” She found herself doing just that in her breakout film, “The Last Picture Show.” This reminded her of what the poet Wisława Szymborska wrote about the soul: “Joy and sorrow / aren’t two different feelings for it.”
Burstyn starred in “Alice” in 1974, alongside Kris Kristofferson, who insisted that he couldn’t act or sing. “What is it you do?” she remembered asking him. “I am a poet,” he answered. In her book, she included a sampling of his lyrics. (“He’s a walkin’ contradiction, partly truth and partly fiction.”) Did any poems remind her of starring in “The Exorcist”? Not quite, she said, but the film does open with the Muslim call to prayer. In the seventies, inspired by an etching of a chanting muezzin in her copy of “The Rubáiyát of Omar Khayyám,” she began travelling the Middle East. In Iran, she visited the Tomb of Hafez. “People come reading Hafez’s poetry,” she said, recalling a path lined with roses “about at nose height, so as you’re walking you’re sniffing a different rose each step.”
In 1995, she was in “How to Make an American Quilt”; her co-star Maya Angelou would recite poems for her. “The makeup department threw up their hands, because I had to be completely re-made up after lunch every day from crying in Maya’s trailer,” Burstyn recalled. “So then I snuck in and brought Q-tips.” She leaned back in her chair and mimed holding the cotton tips to her eyes. “I would lay down in her trailer with Q-tips and go, ‘O.K., hi [TRUNCATED]
